sentences.text: What was it-- uh-- Judg-- yes.
But--
The courtroom-- it was the very same courtroom.
But the-- of course, filmmakers have to have their own script.
[LAUGHS] And they have to have their own characters.
And I personally was not very well satisfied with, with the picture.
Now, other people may have had different--
It-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it wasn't what I had experienced.
Oh, that's hard to describe.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You went into the courtroom.
To the left were the-- was the prisoner dock-- docket.
And immediately adjacent to them was the place where the interpreters worked There were eight interpreters.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And across from the prisoners was the bench for the judges.
The eight judges were seated there.
Right in front of the prisoners was there defense-- were their defense lawyers.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] across from the defense lawyers, in front of the judges, were the court reporters and court stenographers.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] At [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] right angle to that, facing-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] let's put it this way-- there was the prosecution.
The person who was the prosecutor at any particular moment had the judges on his right side and the prisoners, or the accused, on the left side.
Behind him were the various tables and desks where the prosecution sat.
Each prosecution had their own table.
And-- and behind those is where the, the observers were-- they-- the press, on the bottom floor the press, and the visitors' gallery-- gallery.
I wasn't in the court all the time.
I was in court only when needed.
But sometimes, I sat with the Russian prosecution.

You will have a picture later on where you see me with Colonel Petrovski, and sometimes in the projection booth, where I had projections to do, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sometimes at the American prosecution staff table.
So wherever evidence was needed where they had it, that's where I was.
Often.
Let's see if memory [LAUGHS] last that long.

Of course, there was Goering, Hess, Kaltenbrunner, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] von Ribbentrop, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Keitel, Jodl, Donitz, Raeder, von Papen, Schacht, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Speer, Streicher, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Funk, Frank, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Baldur von Schirach.
And there's still, like, five.
Uh-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the one who succeeded Goebbels.
I can't think of-- can't think of-- this is the one that succeeded Goebbels as the propaganda minister.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] All right, now.
Yeah.
As we go.
A moment of satisfaction, [LAUGH] Real satisfaction.
Well, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] like-- really like ordinary human beings.
I mean, just looking at them doesn't mean too much.
The military and the navy people were in their uniforms.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Goering was in a uniform.
But it was not complete.
It was--
The other ones were dressed in civilian clothes.
No, he was in the room where we worked He, he never-- he wa-- he had to go into the courtroom once, as far as I know.
He told me he had to go-- this guard took him there-- to swear that the evidence that he-- that was from him was true.
Yeah.
Well, you had the Russians, the French, the British, and the American.

And then they had subdelegations, like the French had-- I mean, the Russian had, for instance, had the Yugoslav, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and so forth, and wherever the-- but they were part of the Russian prosecution staff.
Yeah-- Justice Jackson.
And--
The ex-senator, Dodd, whose son is senator also in, in Connecticut.
No-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] oh, yes, there was a fellow by the name of-- he handled Rosenberg's case.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Well, it'll come back to me after a while.
Colonel Petrovski, with whom I worked quite a bit and have a story to tell.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Colonel Petrovski and I had a wonderful working relationship.
So nothing personal ever happened.
But he spoke German fluently.
And all our business was actually transacted in German.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when my contract was up, it was just a few weeks before the trials ended.
And my contract was not renewed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I wanted show him how much I had appreciated our working relationship.
So I went and bought him a Parker pencil-- pen and pencil set.
I went to his office and started to talk German and tell him the reason for my coming.
And I handed him this little gift.
And suddenly, he switched from speaking German into speaking Russian through an interpreter.
I didn't speak Russian.
And he-- and, and I, of course, switched to English.
And he told something to his interpreter-- secretary interpreter.
And she left the room.
And a few minutes later, she came back with two volumes of evidence that actually had been presented in court just a few weeks earlier.
And he autographed one of them.
And then he told me, Mr.
Rhee, it has been a pleasure working for you-- with you.
If you ever feel the need-- or feel that I can be of any need or help to you, please do not hesitate to write to me care of the Kremlin.
Of course, I never did.
And that's how I got the Russian evidence.
Oh, under guard, every one.
They had to go up a little elevator-- came up in a little elevator.
And I myself never talked to any of the defendants.
I had nothing to do with them.
I never saw their quarters or anything.
Yeah, some, some of them-- not, not the main-- when Justice Jackson opened the, the trials or-- there was not even standing room.
And you would have had to be airborne to get in.
[LAUGHS]
No, no, no, I was not.
I-- no, not the opening statements so much.
I recall some of the personalized opening statements, like when they had Goering as an individual, or some of that, yes, I--
Well, they wanted to prove that-- they said, we can prove this and this and that and that but any particular moments.
And that, that pertained to all of them, of course.
Yes.
He was smart as a fox.
He wiggled out of the questions.
He had a way of wriggling around the answers.
Not, not really, anymore, but I know that Justice Jackson, at that time, was not very successful in nailing him down.
But the English chief prosecutor, Lord-- I think it was-- Lord Maxwell-Fyfe was his name-- was able to nail him.
He had a different approach.
I mean, I'm not a lawyer, and-- but he had a different way of, let's call it, call it, nailing him down.
Not-- well, we had some business with them.
But I do not recall their names now.
This is 50 years ago.
[LAUGHS]
Yeah, I mentioned-- well, this was a witness-- [?
Olindorf. ?] And I remember one particular incident.
This, I did not witness.
But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Hess-- Rudolf Hess's defense had been trying to prove to the court that he was insane-- that he could not be held accountable for the things that were done.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they had some psychologists in there, or psychiatrists-- whatever you call them-- on the defense.
And they always pleaded that he was innocent.
He should be let go.
And after all the talking was over, Hess gets up-- or got up-- and said, I am perfectly sane.
I want to stand to stick to my buddies with whom I have worked all my life.
And I accept all the responsibilities.
And that was a bombshell.
[LAUGHS]
No, I said I, I, I wasn't in there at that time.
But I was told.
No-- well, Lord Maxwell-Fyfe and Justice Jackson and Dodd, and the other one-- there is one that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I learned recently has died since.
He was on the prosecution staff.
But his name escapes me.
But I have it there.
No, not anything outstanding that--
I think--
I-- each one had at least one defense lawyer.
Some of them had two.
But just how many, I, I do not recall.
No-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] no, I, I really don't--
Well, we were notified in advance-- not just call me in, no.
They, they said, tomorrow such and such a thing is going to happen.
And we have to have this and this ready, and--
Well, we--
We-- there's-- I didn't show so much, except that I-- like, the lampshades, I actually put them into the courtroom.
I did show stills on the projector.
And I did show films on the projector.
And we also made up organizational charts to show how the-- any given unit had been organized and who was the head of it.
So we could nail on the, the defendant with it.
I think this was against-- who was in the east?
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I have some on the pictures, there, but I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] whether it was-- was it Sauckel?
Or was it Frank?
I, I really don't recall.
But those were the things we had to--
They, they were done by people who knew how to do these things.
And we got them into court and [INAUDIBLE].
By, by the prosecution, yes.
Right.
To display them, yes, against the wall, and things like that.

Pictures, movies taken by, let's say, army-- US Army Signal Corps, of the liberation, of some camps, and of the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] burial, if you want to call it-- such digging trenches and then push the bodies in with bulldozers and have lime put on top of them and then cover them up again.
The lime, of course, was to make the bodies disintegrate faster.
That was US Army-- the American--
US Army Signal Corps.
We were provided, yes.
Yeah.
Yes, this was actually shown.
Yes-- Field Marshal Paulus, who had defended Stalingrad.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there were, as I remember, around 300,000 German troops there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when the Russian came back to attack af-after they had defended Moscow and the Germans were forced to withdraw in the north, the-- they had more troops available.
And they, they attacked Stalingrad.
And von Paulus [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was-- saw what the situation was.
And being a, an army guy, a military man, he asked Hitler-- now this is his statement that I heard, myself.
He asked Hitler to surrender the troops, to save the lives.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And Hitler's response was to send him a pistol to commit suicide.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And of course, 300,000 German guys lost their lives there.
But von Paulus got out.
And he was a very weak, old man when I saw him in court, walked on a cane, and very skinny, and very--
Von F-- V-- you want to spell it?
V-O-N is one word.
That's the German title for the nobility.
And Paulus is P-A-U-L-U-S.
He was a German, yes.
[INAUDIBLE] Or was a general?
But I think I-- somehow, it sticks in my mind that he was a field marshal.
Yes.
Well, other than that they [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] fooled the guards here with that little ID deal that I told, I never met many of them, really.
They-- but the only thing I recall is-- this was the Russian media-- they wanted to take a pictures of Hoffmann.
And they came in with a captain, a Russian [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] correspondent-- correspondent-- and somebody else.
And they took a picture.
And I happen to be in that picture.
So I can say I appeared in [LAUGHS]
Well, some of it, as I said, like the evidence in Yugoslavia was actually done by the Germans themselves.
The German photographers took the pictures of the atrocities, made hundreds and hundreds of prints, and circulated them through the population to keep the population in a state of being scared.
So that evidence was, of course, easy to pick up by the Yugoslav prosecution.
Because there were hundreds of them-- thousands of them-- available.
Then the each individual armies had their-- excuse me-- their signal corps.
When they came-- excuse me-- came to camp or something, they took pictures with it.

Then I had this one-- I remember one was done by a, a German amateur movie guy who [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] showed the, the way the prisoners were received at a concentration camp-- how they were-- the women were pulled naked by their hair and so forth and so on.
You have seen these little stills that I have of there-- of them.
That's another one.

That was only a very short-- I don't think the whole thing lasted eight minute. and things like that, we, uh-- then also, I remember once I was sent to Berlin to pick up evidence that had been gathered there and bring it back to--
Well, that was documents and some film.
I know I had to go there.
And--
How, how, the, the Russians themselves authen-- authenticated it, I really don't know.
But it, it, it was official.
I mean, it's--
Well, we got it.
And once it got into the court's hands, I don't know.
We didn't store it.
When we had some, we came up with it.
We notified the particular persons who needed it that we had some.
Oh, yes, it was.
In fact, I was practically the last man on the line to get it.
Where it was kept before that, I, I really don't know.
Because--
After [INAUDIBLE].
I don't know what happened.
I guess the either prosecution or maybe the defense got copies of it also.
I, I really don't know.
Well, some of it I couldn't believe.
I mean, just as you having seen some of it, it, it leaves you a little numb, to say the least.
No other than I like I want the world to know what happened and not to be covered up by some guys-- the so-called revisionists-- that say these things never happened.
Oh, yeah, a few thousand might have died.
They got sick or this and that.
But-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the things like that never happened.
The Germans were much too cultured to do things like that.
No.
No, never.
No.
I had already left, you remember.
I-- my contract was up and so on.
Well, first, I came in as a GI.
And about three, four months later, I took my discharge and then-- in Bamberg-- and then got my was it-- civilian status.
And I forgot how long my contract-- I have the paper there, but I never looked at it again.
But it was up a few weeks before the actual trials were over.
No, I, I didn't.
I felt that I don't-- I had enough.
Yeah.
How-- I took lots of pictures clandestinely.
And the way I did that-- I was not supposed to take any photographs in the courtroom.
But a photograph-- photographer friend of mine took my pictures-- my, my, my camera into the courtroom.
I walked into the courtroom.
And once inside, of course, I wasn't searched.
And, well, he handed me back my camera.
And that's how, how I got the pictures.
